title: Stained glass papercut
subtitle: Cutting a black-paper outline and backing it with coloured tissue
excerpt: A stained glass papercut replicates the visual logic of leaded stained glass in paper: a black paper sheet is cut to leave a framework of interconnected outlines, and translucent coloured tissue or cellophane is glued behind the cut-out spaces. Held to the light, the coloured areas glow through the black framework. The technique requires accurate knife work and a design that observes the same paper-bridge rules as any single-sheet cut.
sourceNotes: Technique synthesised from general two-layer papercutting practice and from the decorative arts tradition of translucent paper lantern and window-picture making, found across European and East Asian craft traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: In a stained glass papercut, the uncut portions of the black paper that form the outlines and divisions between colour areas. Corresponds to the lead strips that hold glass panes together in real stained glass.

content.text: Stained glass papercuts work because the coloured backing layer is visible only through the cut spaces in the black top layer. The black paper acts as the

: the structural outline that separates and contains the colours. The thicker the leading lines, the more robust the cut and the more overtly decorative the result. Thin leading reads as a fine outline drawing; thick leading reads as bold, simplified forms. Design the width of the leading lines before beginning to cut.

attrs.heading: Safety: cutting tools
attrs.body: Change the blade frequently. Black card is denser than plain cartridge paper and a dull blade requires more force, which increases the risk of the knife jumping the cut line. Hold the paper firmly on the cutting mat but keep fingers clear of the knife path. Work at a well-lit surface; the light box beneath the work will help, but overhead light is also needed for seeing fine detail on the black surface.

items.name: Black card or paper, 160-200 gsm
items.description: Heavier than standard paper: the increased weight gives clean-edged cuts without feathering. Avoid card with a glossy coating, as it reflects glare from the light box and makes the template hard to see.

items.name: Coloured tissue paper or coloured cellophane
items.description: Cut slightly larger than each opening. Tissue paper gives a warm, diffused colour; cellophane is more saturated and glossy. Mix the two for different effects within one piece.

items.name: Tracing paper (slug: tracing-paper)
items.description: For transferring the design to the back of the black card.

items.name: Light box, A4 (slug: light-box-a4)
items.description: Used both for tracing the design onto the black card and for checking the cut work in progress: the coloured areas will already begin to show their effect on the light box before the backing is applied.

content.text: Draw the design on plain paper at the intended finished size. Mark the areas to be cut away with light shading. The minimum leading width: the thinnest black strip between two openings: should be at least 2-3 mm for card at 160 gsm; thinner strips crack when flexed. Simple geometric and botanical forms work well because their curves are predictable. Complex figurative designs are possible but require more careful engineering of the leading connections.
Transferring and cutting the black layer

Trace the design onto tracing paper and transfer it to the back of the black card by re-tracing with a hard pencil. Place the card face-down on the cutting mat and cut from the back, following the transferred lines. Cutting from the back means any slight ragged edges from the knife entry will be on the back face, not the front. Cut interior areas first, then the outer border. On the light box, check the cut in progress: any unintended connections or missed cuts are visible immediately.

Turn the cut black layer face-down. Cut patches of coloured tissue or cellophane slightly larger than each opening. Apply a thin line of PVA or glue stick to the leading strips immediately surrounding each opening and press the colour patch over it. Work one colour area at a time and allow each patch to tack before moving to the next. Once all colour patches are applied, press a clean backing sheet of white cartridge over the whole piece to protect the tissue and give it rigidity.

The piece reads best when backlit. Tape it to a window or place it over a light box. For framing, use a frame with a backing board that can be removed so the piece can be lit from behind. The colours are most saturated and luminous when the piece is held to natural daylight; artificial light gives a warmer, slightly yellowed result.
